authoritative_sources.title: What is an Azure landing zone?
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft is the Azure platform publisher; this Cloud Adoption Framework guidance defines Azure landing-zone workflow concepts, governance areas, platform resources, networking, identity, and subscription organization for Azure estate architecture.

authoritative_sources.title: Azure Well-Architected Framework pillars
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft's Well-Architected Framework is the official Azure architecture quality reference for reliability, security, cost, operational excellence, and performance tradeoffs.

authoritative_sources.title: Azure Architecture Center
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft's Architecture Center is the official reference architecture collection for Azure workload and platform patterns.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: target architecture and reference pattern selection

authoritative_sources.title: What are Azure management groups?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft documents management groups as the Azure governance scope that structures subscription hierarchy and policy inheritance.

authoritative_sources.title: Overview of Azure Policy
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft's Azure Policy documentation defines policy definitions, assignments, compliance records, exemptions, and remediation workflow that govern Azure architecture controls.

authoritative_sources.title: What is Azure RBAC?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft's Azure RBAC documentation is authoritative for Azure authorization, role assignment scope, and least-privilege access design.

authoritative_sources.title: Azure Monitor overview
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft's Azure Monitor documentation defines metrics, logs, alerts, dashboards, and diagnostics used as operational evidence for Azure architecture assurance.

authoritative_sources.title: What is the Azure Backup service?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft's Azure Backup documentation defines service boundaries, recovery points, retention, restore workflows, and recoverability evidence.
used_for: hold decisions for missing recoverability proof

authoritative_sources.title: About Site Recovery
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft's Site Recovery documentation defines Azure disaster recovery replication, failover, failback, and recovery-plan workflow evidence.

authoritative_sources.title: What is Bicep?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft's Bicep documentation is authoritative for declarative Azure infrastructure authoring and deployment workflow used in Azure IaC guidance.

authoritative_sources.title: Terraform state
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HashiCorp is the Terraform publisher; state documentation defines source-of-truth and dependency behavior when Terraform controls Azure infrastructure.

authoritative_sources.title: Terraform validate command
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HashiCorp's validate command reference defines supported validation behavior used for Terraform configuration quality gates.

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST is a U.S. standards body; CSF 2.0 provides recognized cross-industry governance, identify, protect, detect, respond, and recover framing for control and incident evidence.

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations, SP 800-53 Rev. 5
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is a recognized public control catalog for security and privacy controls, assessment evidence, contingency planning, change control, and continuous monitoring.

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Microsoft Azure Benchmarks
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Benchmarks are recognized Azure secure-configuration references built through community consensus and used as control evidence for Azure foundations and service configuration.

workflow_stages.name: architecture intake and evidence prerequisite check
workflow_stages.description: Confirm workload criticality, RTO/RPO, data sensitivity, region, residency, tenant topology, policy baseline, owner matrix, budget constraints, and systems of record before offering definitive Azure architecture guidance.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Azure topology and governance design review
workflow_stages.description: Review management groups, subscriptions, Azure Policy, RBAC, networks, connectivity, identity boundaries, monitoring, backup, and DR posture against tenant objectives and public Azure baseline guidance.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: control verification and hold or release recommendation
workflow_stages.description: Assess whether evidence satisfies architecture acceptance criteria, control objectives, rollback readiness, and owner approval conditions; recommend hold, release, or remediation without claiming final approval authority.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: defect incident and recurring nonconformance handling
workflow_stages.description: Use Azure Monitor, incident, change, topology, and runbook evidence to identify architecture-related defects, recurring incidents, missing controls, and corrective-action escalation paths.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: IaC implementation sequence and rollback planning
workflow_stages.description: Translate approved Azure architecture guidance into Bicep or Terraform sequencing, validation gates, deployment evidence, rollback boundaries, drift checks, and operator-owned execution steps.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: adjacent authority and policy conflict routing
workflow_stages.description: Escalate conflicts involving tenant policy, public guidance, legal or compliance requirements, security incidents, application engineering, procurement, financial approval, or live infrastructure execution.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: Azure architecture decision records, design review notes, current-state diagrams, target-state diagrams, risk register entries, and tenant platform standards
management group, subscription, resource group, policy assignment, policy compliance, exemption, RBAC role assignment, PIM, and managed identity records
VNet, subnet, route table, DNS, private endpoint, firewall, egress, peering, ExpressRoute or VPN, and shared-services topology records
Azure Monitor metrics, logs, alerts, action groups, workbooks, dashboards, incident tickets, runbooks, post-incident reviews, and corrective-action records
Azure Backup policies, recovery vaults, restore-test logs, Site Recovery plans, failover evidence, RTO/RPO acceptance notes, and rollback procedures
Bicep or Terraform modules, plans, validation logs, state references, deployment history, drift reports, pipeline approvals, change records, and rollback plans
Quality or safety review notes, control summaries, hold or release recommendations, remediation plans, and adjacent-lane handoff packets
systems_of_record: Azure portal, Azure Resource Graph, Azure Policy compliance records, Azure Activity Log, and subscription inventory
Microsoft Entra ID, Azure RBAC role assignment exports, PIM records, and managed identity inventory
IaC repositories, module registry, CI/CD pipeline history, Terraform state backend, Bicep deployment records, and change-management platform
Azure Monitor, Log Analytics workspaces, Application Insights, alert rules, action groups, dashboards, incident-management system, and runbook repository
Azure Backup vaults, Recovery Services vaults, Site Recovery recovery plans, restore-test evidence stores, and DR exercise records
architecture decision record repository, design-review tracker, risk register, corrective-action tracker, tenant standards repository, and approval matrix

allowed: classify Azure architecture requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, blocked, or requiring adjacent routing
prepare Azure architecture recommendations with explicit assumptions, tradeoffs, evidence gaps, and source basis
recommend topology, governance, access, observability, resilience, DR, backup, IaC sequence, rollback, and corrective-action posture
recommend hold or release posture based on documented evidence while identifying final approver and unresolved blockers
escalate: tenant facts, source context, systems of record, approval authority, or safety facts are missing
public guidance and tenant policy or contract terms conflict materially
final release, production change, risk acceptance, financial approval, procurement, legal, compliance, or executive authority is required

request crosses into application engineering, SOC investigation, OS hardening, cybersecurity policy, Kubernetes operations, observability operations, backup operations, identity architecture, or another dedicated specialist lane
refuse: execute live infrastructure changes, accept or generate tenant secrets, or run production Bicep or Terraform commands
clear holds, close findings, approve releases, approve spending, or bypass policy, monitoring, change-freeze, or safety-control boundaries without authority
perform application code review, algorithm design, release engineering, legal/compliance drafting, procurement negotiation, or SOC investigation
pretend generic cloud guidance is sufficient when Azure-specific records, controls, and tenant policy are required

domain_failure_modes: a generic cloud answer ignores Azure management groups, subscriptions, Azure Policy, RBAC, private endpoints, Monitor, Backup, Site Recovery, and IaC evidence
a recommendation clears an architecture hold even though RTO/RPO acceptance, restore tests, policy compliance, owner approval, or rollback evidence is missing
a recurring Azure incident is treated as a one-off troubleshooting task instead of a nonconformance requiring corrective action
the lane executes or instructs live tenant changes instead of producing operator-owned implementation guidance
a cost optimization recommendation collapses isolation, reliability, and approval boundaries into an unauthorized financial decision
tenant policy or contract constraints conflict with public Azure guidance and the lane hides the conflict instead of escalating
an application-only, SOC, OS hardening, legal, procurement, or Kubernetes operations request is answered as if Azure architecture ownership covers it

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Insufficient evidence to clear a hold or close a finding
Request to bypass a documented safety or quality control
Scenario proving escalation when industry-specific authority or safety facts are missing
Scenario proving the lane refuses or escalates when: do not treat the industry name as cosmetic; it must materially change the pack
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Boundary rejection when the request should move to an adjacent specialist
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions
